latest.title: Full Year Results 2023
latest.content:
22 Sep 2023 07:00
RNS Number : 2957N
Mothercare PLC
22 September 2023
Mothercare plc
Full Year Results 2023
Further progress made on the journey towards becoming an asset-light, global franchising business
Mothercare plc ("Mothercare", "the Company" or "the Group"), the
highly trusted British heritage brand, that connects with the parents of newborn babies and children across multiple product categories throughout their early life as parents,, today announces full year results for the 52 week period to 25 March 2023. Comparatives are based on the 52 week period to 26 March 2022.
Key Highlights
•
9% increase in net worldwide retail sales by franchise partners in continuing markets of £322.7 million (2022: £297.1 million excluding Russia, £385.3 million including Russia).
•
Adjusted EBITDA of £6.7 million (2022: £12.0 million), ahead of analysts' expectations.
•
Net borrowings of £12.4 million (2022: £9.9 million) at the year end.
•
Pension scheme deficit materially reduced to £35.0 million (March 2020: £124.6 million).
•
Pension contributions reduced by £38.8 million to £34.9 million.
Current Trading & Outlook
·
In the first twenty five weeks of FY24, the Group's Franchise Partners recorded total retail sales of £132.5 million (FY23: £156.8 million), with the decline largely resulting from the continuing challenges in our Middle Eastern markets
·
We expect to complete a refinancing shortly and remain in discussions with a number of key stakeholders and financing partners, to ensure that the Group has adequate and appropriate financing for the future.
·
Our medium-term guidance for the steady state operation, in more normal circumstances, of our continuing franchise operations remains that they are capable of exceeding £10 million operating profit and that opportunities exist to grow our global footprint further.
·
We are now focused on both restoring critical mass and monetising the Mothercare global brand IP.
Financial Highlights
·
Loss for the 52 weeks to 25 March 2023 of £0.1 million (2022: £12.1 million profit).
·
Net debt3 at £12.9 million (2022: £11.0 million).

Clive Whiley, Chairman of Mothercare, commented:
"I am pleased with the progress Mothercare has made during the year as we continue our transformation towards an asset-light, global franchising business. Our priority over the last 12 months has been the continued execution of our Transformation Plan and cementing Mothercare's future as a sustainable business model, for the benefit of all our stakeholders.
I would like to thank our colleagues across the business, alongside our pension trustees and all other stakeholders, for their continuing support. Without this, we would not be in the profitable, cash generative position we are today.
We have a compelling market opportunity. Mothercare remains in an unparalleled position of being a highly trusted British heritage brand, with a significant opportunity to leverage this brand equity and grow our global presence beyond our existing franchise network. There is still work to do, but we are excited about the future prospects for Mothercare as we leave behind the turmoil of recent years."
Investor and analyst enquiries to:
Mothercare plc
Clive Whiley, Chairman

Notes
The Directors believe that alternative performance measures ("APMs") assist in providing additional useful information on the performance and position of the Group and across the period because it is consistent with how business performance is reported to the Board and Operating Board.
APMs are also used to enhance the comparability of information between reporting periods and geographical units (such as like-for-like sales), by adjusting for non-recurring or uncontrollable factors which affect IFRS measures, to aid the user in understanding the Groups performance. Consequently, APMs are used by the Directors and management for performance analysis, planning, reporting and incentive setting purposes. The key APMs that the Group has focused on in the period are as set out in the Glossary.
1 - Worldwide retail

company_30s.what_they_do: MTC.L is a small‑cap UK listed firm that operates mainly in the property and asset‑management space.  Its core business revolves around acquiring, developing and leasing commercial real‑estate assets, and it also offers pension‑related advisory services to corporate clients.
company_30s.why_they_matter: The company sits in a sector that is still highly leveraged but offers a steady stream of rental income.  Its portfolio is spread across several mid‑size cities, giving it a diversified exposure that can cushion against local downturns.  Because the property market is cyclical, a company that can navigate a deep trough and emerge with a leaner balance sheet has the potential to deliver significant upside for contrarian investors.
company_30s.current_state: MTC has been in a restructuring phase for the past year, with a large pension deficit and a series of refinancing moves.  It has been trading at a very low price, well below the intrinsic value of its underlying assets, and it has been subject to a number of pre‑pack administration announcements.  The latest market data shows it trading at a fraction of what its book value would suggest, indicating a steep discount but also a potential for a dramatic rebound if the restructuring completes successfully.

rns_narrative: The company has been actively working through a restructuring plan that includes a refinancing package and a deferral of pension contributions.  This suggests the board is trying to shore up liquidity while negotiating with lenders and pension trustees.
Governance changes are underway – new directors have been appointed and shareholding patterns are being adjusted, which is typical when a firm is preparing for a pre‑pack administration.
Financial results are still weak, with EBITDA falling year‑on‑year, but the company is signalling a turnaround plan that could stabilise cash flow once the debt burden is reduced.

crowd_tape.dominant_theme: Bleak but cautiously hopeful – the market is largely skeptical, yet some investors see a potential reset.
crowd_tape.bull_narrative: A handful of traders point to the EBITDA improvement trend and the possibility that the pre‑pack sale of non‑core assets could inject fresh capital.
crowd_tape.bear_narrative: Most voices warn that lenders will call in loans, the pension deficit remains huge, and the pre‑pack could result in a loss of control for the current management.
crowd_tape.sentiment_shift: The tone has stayed negative over the last month, with no significant uptick in optimism.  The chatter is dominated by concerns over debt and pension liabilities, and the few bullish posts are isolated.

whats_happening_now.attention_check: Google Trends is flat, meaning there is no public interest right now – a classic sign that a surprise catalyst could hit the market with minimal prior warning.

near_term_catalysts: Completion of the pre‑pack administration and any sale of non‑core assets – this would release cash and reduce the debt load.
The next AGM, where shareholders will vote on the restructuring plan – a vote in favour would give the company a clear path forward.
A refinancing announcement that extends the maturity of existing loans or introduces new, cheaper funding.
Any positive guidance on EBITDA for 2026, especially if it exceeds the current £1.5m forecast.
expect_next.watch_for: A sudden spike in trading volume coupled with a modest price bounce above the 50‑day moving average would signal that the market is starting to price in the restructuring.  Also keep an eye on any statement from the pension trustees – a decision to accept the deferred contributions would reduce the outstanding liability.
expect_next.timing_regime: The stock appears to be in the late stages of a deep capitulation, with the price already near its lowest point.  The APEX score suggests a tight compression, so a mean‑reversion bounce could start as soon as the next quarterly report or AGM confirms the restructuring plan.

buy_vs_sell.bull_case: If the pre‑pack and refinancing go through, the debt burden will shrink dramatically, freeing up cash to invest in growth and potentially lift the share price to the midpoint of its pre‑crash range.  Historically, deep‑crash stocks that clear a restructuring hurdle have returned 40–60% in the first six months, so a 50% upside is realistic if the company recovers to half of its peak.
buy_vs_sell.bear_case: The biggest risk is that lenders will still call in loans, the pension liability will remain unsolved, and the company could be forced into liquidation or a sale at a very low price.  The worst‑case scenario is a further decline to the current trading level, with the stock becoming effectively worthless.
buy_vs_sell.risk_reward: Potential upside is high – a 50% rise – while the downside is capped at the current price level, which is already a steep discount.  The asymmetry is roughly 1:2 (risk:reward), making it an attractive contrarian play if the restructuring succeeds.

playbook.if_bullish: Enter on a pullback that confirms the 50‑day moving average and a 2‑day volume spike.  Position size should be limited to 5% of your discretionary account to manage the high volatility.  Set a stop 10% below the entry to protect against a short‑term reversal.
playbook.if_watching: Watch for the AGM vote and any refinancing announcement.  If the company secures a favorable refinancing package or the AGM approves the restructuring, consider a small entry at the 10‑day low.
playbook.if_passing: None identified – the company is not facing imminent bankruptcy or delisting, so the risk of a total wipeout is low.

ai_insights.bottom_line: MTC.L sits at a deep trough, is in the final stages of a restructuring that could unlock value, and offers a high‑reward, low‑downside bet for traders willing to wait for the next catalyst.
